title: PRD-001: Optimized Tool Installation

overview.description: Optimize tool installation performance through strategic parallelization where safe, batch operations, and parallel pre-flight checks. Critical constraint: Most system package managers use exclusive locking and cannot run concurrently.
goals: Batch system package manager operations for faster installs
Parallel user-space package manager installs (npm, pip, cargo, go)
nonGoals: Parallel downloads with sequential installs (descoped - package managers handle download optimization)

userStories.title: Phase 1: Batch Operations
userStories.description: As a developer, I want tools to be installed in batches per package manager so that installation time is reduced through single dependency resolution and lock acquisition.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Add PackageManager enum if not exists
Group tools by package manager in setup flow
Implement batch_install() for Homebrew (brew install a b c)
Implement batch_install() for apt (apt install -y a b c)
Implement batch_install() for dnf (dnf install -y a b c)
Implement batch_install() for pacman (pacman -S --noconfirm a b c)
Implement batch_install() for apk (apk add a b c)
Implement batch_install() for winget
Implement batch_install() for Chocolatey (choco install a b c -y)
Handle mixed success/failure in batch operations (parse output, retry failed individually)
Add integration tests for batch installation

userStories.title: Phase 2: Parallel Version Checking
userStories.description: As a developer, I want all tool versions checked concurrently before installation so that the installation plan is built faster.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Add rayon dependency to Cargo.toml
Refactor version checking to use par_iter()
Collect all version results before building installation plan
Update progress reporting to show parallel check status
Version checks complete for 10+ tools in ~2 seconds (vs ~10 seconds sequential)

userStories.title: Phase 3: User-Space Parallelization
userStories.description: As a developer, I want npm, pip, cargo, and go installations to run concurrently so that user-space tool installs are faster.
userStories.notes: Implemented with rayon ThreadPool in main.rs, --jobs flag (default 4), --sequential flag, and thread-safe error aggregation

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Identify and categorize user-space package managers (npm, pip, cargo, go)
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Tools with custom installers are categorized separately in ToolGroups.custom_install

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Implement parallel installation with configurable concurrency limit (default 2-4)
Add --jobs flag for user control of concurrency
Handle error aggregation from parallel installs
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Uses Arc<Mutex<Vec>> for thread-safe success/error collectors

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Detect and warn about potential conflicts (npm/pip with --global)
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Not implemented - low priority, user-space PMs rarely conflict

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Add tests for parallel user-space installation

userStories.title: Phase 4: Parallel Downloads
userStories.description: As a developer, I want binary downloads to happen in parallel with sequential installation so that download time is minimized.
userStories.notes: Decision: Will not implement. Parallel/group downloads add significant complexity for minimal benefit. Most tools are installed via package managers (brew, apt, etc.) which handle their own download optimization. Custom installers (rustup, nvm) already use efficient download strategies.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Identify tools using binary downloads
Implement async/parallel download for binaries
Queue sequential installation after downloads complete
Add tests for parallel download functionality

userStories.title: Progress Reporting & UX
userStories.description: As a user, I want real-time progress updates during installation so that I know what is happening and can estimate completion time.
userStories.notes: Progress reporting implemented with batch status, parallel job counts, and --sequential flag

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Show real-time status of batch operations
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Displays 'Installing N custom tools with M parallel jobs' and individual tool status

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Maintain deterministic output ordering
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Use --sequential flag for deterministic output; parallel mode outputs as completed

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Support --sequential flag for debugging
Display which tools are being installed in parallel vs sequentially
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Shows 'Installing N tools with M parallel jobs' for parallel mode

userStories.title: Dependency Ordering
userStories.description: As a developer, I want tool dependencies respected so that tools like nvm are installed before node.
userStories.notes: Implemented depends_on field in ToolSpec, order_tools_by_dependencies() with topological sort, and added dependencies to node->nvm, python->pyenv, ruby->rbenv

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Define tool dependency relationships
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Added depends_on: Option<&'static [&'static str]> field to ToolSpec and define_tool! macro

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Ensure dependency ordering is respected in batch operations
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Implemented order_tools_by_dependencies() using Kahn's topological sort algorithm in main.rs setup flow

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Add tests for dependency ordering
acceptanceCriteria.notes: Added 6 tests: test_get_tool_dependencies_no_deps, test_tool_has_dependencies, test_order_tools_no_dependencies, test_order_tools_empty_input, test_order_tools_single_tool, test_order_tools_preserves_versions, test_order_tools_case_insensitive

functionalRequirements.description: Detect and group tools by package manager
Use batch install where supported (brew install a b c)
functionalRequirements.notes: All major package managers supported via batch_install()

functionalRequirements.description: Parallel version checking for all tools before installation
Parallel user-space installs (npm, pip, cargo, go)
functionalRequirements.notes: Custom installers run in parallel via rayon ThreadPool

functionalRequirements.description: Parallel binary downloads with sequential installation
functionalRequirements.notes: Will not implement - descoped. Package managers handle download optimization; complexity not justified for marginal benefit.
Shows batch status, parallel job counts

functionalRequirements.description: Respect tool dependencies (e.g., nvm before node)
functionalRequirements.notes: Implemented via depends_on field and order_tools_by_dependencies()

nonGoals: Concurrent system package manager installs (brew, apt, dnf, pacman, winget use exclusive locking)
Concurrent modifications to shared resources (PATH, registry)

technicalConsiderations.details: All system package managers (Homebrew, apt, dnf, pacman, apk, winget, Chocolatey) use exclusive locking. Concurrent installs will fail or block.
Only version checking, binary downloads, user-space package managers (npm, pip, cargo, go), and post-install hooks can be safely parallelized.
All major package managers support batch install except cargo. winget is sequential internally even with batch syntax.
Cargo builds may compete for CPU/memory. Default to conservative concurrency (2-4) for user-space parallelization.
Batch operations may have partial failures. Must parse output to identify failed packages and optionally retry individually.

filesToModify.changes: Add batch install functions, PackageManager grouping
Refactor setup loop for batching and parallelization
Add batch and parallel installation tests

successMetrics: Version check (10 tools): ~10 sec → ~2 sec (Phase 2 complete)
Mixed install (brew + npm + pip): ~3 min → ~1 min (Full implementation)

risks.mitigation: Parse output to identify failed packages, retry individually

implementationNotes.phase1Summary: Implemented batch installation for all major package managers (Homebrew, apt, dnf, yum, zypper, pacman, apk, winget, Chocolatey). Tools are grouped by package manager before installation, and batch commands are used for efficiency. Partial failure handling retries failed packages individually.
